title: Bag of Words: Counting Words as Features
concept: Bag of Words is the simplest way to vectorize text: build a vocabulary of every unique word across all documents, then represent each document as a vector counting how many times each word appears — with word order thrown away completely.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What did you learn had to happen before any classifier could work with raw text?

options.text: The words had to be translated into some kind of numeric representation
The text had to be deleted and replaced with a photo
Nothing — classifiers can read raw sentences directly

exercises.explain: Exactly! Text has to become numbers first. Today you'll learn the simplest way anyone ever came up with to do that.

cards.title: Dump every word into a bag
cards.body: Imagine grabbing every single word from 100 customer reviews and dumping them all into one giant bag. Then you just... count. How many times does "great" show up? How many times does "broken"? That's genuinely almost the whole idea.

cards.title: What is Bag of Words?
cards.body: Bag of Words is the simplest text vectorization technique. First, you build a vocabulary: a list of every unique word that appears anywhere across all your documents. Then, each document becomes a vector — a list of numbers counting how many times each vocabulary word appears in that specific document.

cards.title: Scrabble tiles in a bag
cards.body: Think of a sentence as a set of Scrabble tiles laid out in careful order to spell something. Bag of Words dumps those exact same tiles into a bag and shakes it. You can still count how many of each letter tile you have — but the careful order that spelled out a real sentence is completely gone.

cards.title: Two tiny documents, one vocabulary
cards.body: Doc A: "the cat sat". Doc B: "the dog sat". Combined vocabulary: [the, cat, sat, dog]. Doc A becomes the vector [1, 1, 1, 0]. Doc B becomes [1, 0, 1, 1]. Every document is now a row of numbers, exactly what a classifier needs.

cards.title: Order is not preserved — at all
cards.body: It's easy to assume Bag of Words keeps some sense of a sentence's structure. It doesn't. "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" produce the exact same vector, since both contain the same three words the same number of times. Very different meanings, identical numbers.

cards.title: Sentence vs. Bag of Words Vector
cards.body: What survives the translation, and what doesn't:

right.text:
[the:1, dog:1, sat:1]
Order is gone
Just word counts

exercises.prompt: In Bag of Words, what does the vocabulary consist of?

options.text: Every unique word that appears anywhere across all the documents
Only the words that appear in every single document
A random sample of 10 words per document

exercises.explain: Right — the vocabulary is built from every distinct word across the whole collection of documents, no matter how rare.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Bag of Words keeps track of the order words appear in within a sentence.
exercises.explain: False — that's the whole trade-off of Bag of Words. It's simple and fast, but word order is thrown away entirely.

exercises.sentence: In Bag of Words, each unique word across all documents becomes an entry in the ___.
exercises.explain: The vocabulary is the master list of unique words that gives every document's count vector its structure.

exercises.prompt: Two reviews say "great service, terrible food" and "terrible service, great food." What does Bag of Words do with them?

options.text: Produces the exact same vector for both, since they contain the identical words the identical number of times
Correctly flags them as opposite opinions about service and food

exercises.explain: Since Bag of Words only counts words and ignores order, these two very different-sounding reviews end up as identical vectors.

exercises.prompt: "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" describe completely different events. After converting both to Bag of Words vectors, what would you find?

options.text: Identical vectors, because both sentences contain exactly the same words the same number of times — order isn't captured
Completely different vectors, because Bag of Words tracks the position of every word
Bag of Words would refuse to process one of the sentences
The vectors would differ only in length

exercises.explain: Exactly right! Bag of Words counts words, full stop — it has no idea which word came first. That's a real limitation, and it's exactly what you'll start fixing later this unit.
